questions.prompt: What is the bottom-up order of the testing levels?
questions.explanation: From smallest scope up: unit, integration, system, then user acceptance.
A unit test exercises a single function/component in isolation, usually written by developers.

questions.prompt: Two services each work alone but break when combined. Which level targets this?
questions.explanation: Integration testing checks the seams and contracts between separately-built pieces.

questions.prompt: Who typically owns User Acceptance Testing?
questions.explanation: UAT is the business/users confirming the system meets their real needs before go-live.

questions.prompt: Where is a logic bug in a single function cheapest to catch?
questions.explanation: Lower levels are cheaper and more precise; a unit test pinpoints the function fastest.

questions.prompt: The manual tester's primary home level is usually:
questions.explanation: Manual testers most often work at the system level, exercising the whole assembled product against requirements.
